evidence: "Into The Wind" puts you in the pilot's seat... taking you on a spiritually calming, yet thrilling white-knuckle ride.
I dig Steve Martin from back in the days of "The Jerk", "Planes Trains & Automobiles", "Parenthood" and "Dirty Rotten Scoundrels"...
The movie starts off slowly. Establishing the characters is a must... and Eastwood knows his trade.
The story is all dialogue (and it's none of that trademark witty Coen dialogue we've come to love and expect).
How long can we sit and watch the main character drive his car, sit on the sofa, read his mail, stare out the window (without dialogue or any background music)?
The cinematography, the music, the learn-to approach... the entire journey is an A+ effort.
The film is shot in beautiful anamorphic widescreen accompanied with 5.1 digital sound.
I really had to scratch my head with his "Lost In Translation" (2003).
I dig Steve Martin from back in the days of "The Jerk", "Planes Trains & Automobiles"...
The special effects were awesome. The huge waves coming into NYC looked incredible.
The story starts off with Maggie wandering into the gym, trying to work out, looking to be trained.
The film conveys the message that 'anyone' with a sense of adventure can take to the sky.

summary: The user enjoys documentaries and dramas with high production quality, but shows a strong preference for simple narratives and slow pacing. They have mixed feelings about brand loyalty, indicating a nuanced appreciation for certain actors and directors, while also valuing emotional resonance in their viewing experiences.
